text: The surgeon uses sharp scissors to release adhesions, applying adequate counter-traction to expose the tissue plane during the adhesiolysis procedure.
The surgeon applies diathermy to the adhesions, maintaining a safe distance from the bowel to keep the surgical field clean.

The surgeon releases the omentum adherent to the bowel, revealing no clear plane between the abdominal wall and bowel loop. To prevent intestinal injury, the surgeon dissects one layer above, removing the peritoneum along with the bowel loop. The pre-peritoneal pad of fat is visible, and a patch of peritoneum is removed from the abdominal wall, sometimes including a bit of rectus sheath.
The surgeon uses counter traction to expose the surgical field, revealing another loop of bowel as adhesions are being released.

The surgeon uses a 30-degree telescope and sharp scissors to navigate and dissect adhesions. The approach involves alternating between the left and right sides to safely manage any difficulties encountered during the procedure.

The surgeon divides the sac containing omentum during the ventral hernia repair, ensuring that no bulk of omentum is left within the sac to prevent future complications such as necrosis or sinus formation.
The surgeon removes the entire hernia content, revealing a flattened bowel loop within the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies multiple defects in the surgical field, distinguishing between bowel and hernia sac. The hernia sac is visually confirmed and divided with precision.
The surgeon carefully releases the final adhesion, completing the process of adhesiolysis. The surgical field shows the separation of tissue layers as the adhesion is divided.
